worth_it_explanation: Floor pricing sets the line between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one, so it controls how much revenue a publisher collects from the same inventory. A floor that leans entirely on automation, or a single flat number applied across every placement, format, country, and device, can leave money uncollected or impressions unsold without anyone noticing. The check reviews the floor rules a publisher already runs in Magnite, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price a publisher accepts for an impression sold through Magnite, which sets where the line falls between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one. Magnite is a sell-side platform (SSP), so the publisher controls these floors directly, both inside the Magnite console and through Demand Manager when it manages the header-bidding wrapper. This subcategory is about the floor strategy itself, the values chosen and how they are applied, rather than the wider pricing mechanics that carry them. These checks confirm that the floors reflect the price differences across the publisher's inventory and that any automated floor is a measured choice rather than a default accepted without comparison.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by inventory, format, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all inventory ignores that placements, ad formats, countries, and devices clear at different prices. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies along several lines at once.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's inventory. An above-the-fold display unit on a high-engagement page clears higher than a unit far down a thin page. A video impression clears higher than a small banner. Traffic from a high-spending country clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin, and desktop, mobile web, and in-app inventory each carry their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the strong inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the strong inventory and it sits above the weak inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

Magnite lets a publisher set floors at the level of individual supply rather than one floor for everything. In the Magnite console, the floor is the lowest cost-per-thousand (CPM) the publisher will accept for a piece of supply, and it can be set per ad unit, per format, and per other supply attributes. Where Demand Manager manages the header-bidding wrapper, the underlying Prebid Price Floors module can vary the floor by ad unit, media type, ad size, and geography, so the floor can follow the dimensions that move the clearing price.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One floor value covers all inventory, with no breakout by ad unit, format, geography, or device
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across placements, formats, countries, or devices while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium placements clear at or near the same floor as filler inventory, which suggests the floor is not protecting their value
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by ad unit, format, geography, and device, then set floors that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a homepage or section-front placement usually clears higher than an article-footer unit, video clears higher than display, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by placement, format, and geography so the premium inventory holds its value while the lower-value inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience closer to a purchase and clear higher than editorial pages, and device matters because mobile shoppers and desktop shoppers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent inventory carries a higher floor than the editorial inventory rather than sharing one number.

learn_more.source_title: Module: Price Floors for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed Demand Manager floor configuration documentation lives in the partner portal and the DV+ Resource Center (login required), so the public links above cover the floor concept and the underlying Prebid floors mechanism rather than the step-by-step console setup.

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across ad units, formats, geographies, and device categories to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Open the inventory or supply section of the Magnite console and list the floor (CPM) set against each piece of supply.
Note whether floors are set at the ad-unit and format level or whether one floor is inherited across the whole supply hierarchy with no breakout.
Record which inventory carries a deliberate floor and which simply inherits a parent floor. Inherited floors are the most likely place a flat value is doing work several floors should do.

demand_manager.label: Magnite Demand Manager
steps: If Demand Manager manages the header-bidding wrapper, open its floor or pricing controls and review how the Prebid Price Floors rules are defined.
Note whether floor rules break out by ad unit, media type, ad size, or geography, or whether a single rule applies across all auctions.
Record the dimensions where a floor is differentiated and the dimensions where one value covers everything.

reporting.label: Reporting (Magnite console or ad server)
steps: Build a report broken out by ad unit, then by format, then by country, then by device category, with average CPM and fill rate.
Compare the average clearing price across those dimensions to see how much it varies between strong and weak inventory.
Note any case where clearing prices differ widely but the floor stays the same, which points to inventory that a segmented floor could price more accurately.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across ad units, formats, geographies, and device categories alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by ad unit, format, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across inventory whose value differs by ad unit, format, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by ad unit, format, geography, and device, then setting floors in the Magnite console, or in the Demand Manager floor rules where it runs the wrapper, that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.

audit_checks.title: Dynamic or automated floors are accepted without a fixed-floor baseline
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An automated floor optimizes toward the objective the model was built for, which is yield, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point. Accepting it without comparing it to a fixed-floor baseline leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the automated floor is raising revenue or trading away inventory value on a slice of inventory.

educate.base:
Magnite offers more than one way to set a floor. A fixed floor is a cost-per-thousand (CPM) the publisher chooses and the platform applies as written. A dynamic floor instead hands the floor to a model that adjusts it per auction to balance fill and value, drawing on real-time marketplace signals. Where Demand Manager manages the header-bidding wrapper, the Prebid Price Floors module supports the same split: a static floor the publisher hardcodes, or a dynamic floor fetched from a floor-data service before each auction. Magnite has also moved toward automated floor guidance inside Demand Manager that recommends or sets floors on the publisher's behalf.

The risk is not that automation is wrong. The risk is accepting it without a comparison. A dynamic floor optimizes toward the objective the model was built for, which is yield, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A publisher protecting a premium price point on a sponsorship-adjacent placement, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a direct-sold rate intact, may find the automated floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Without a fixed-floor baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see that happening.

The Prebid floors documentation itself notes that static floors are blunt tools that publishers tend to forget to update, which is the case for dynamic floors in the general run of inventory. That is a reason to test the comparison rather than a reason to skip it. A publisher can run the dynamic floor on a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read revenue, fill rate, and average price across both. That turns the floor choice into a measured result rather than a default that was never questioned.

A few signs point to a dynamic floor being accepted rather than evaluated:
  • Floors run on dynamic or automated pricing across all inventory with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the automated floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • The publisher cannot say why the automated option was chosen beyond it being the default or a recommendation that was accepted
You may want to confirm that the dynamic-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory long enough to compare it against the automated floor on the rest.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements such as a homepage takeover slot or a sponsorship-adjacent unit often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those units while running the dynamic floor elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether automation respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can command higher prices than editorial pages because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare the dynamic floor against a fixed floor on the commercial pages specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Module: Price Floors for Header Bidding
Intro to Floors for Prebid.js and Prebid Server

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed documentation for Demand Manager's dynamic and automated floor guidance is in the partner portal and the DV+ Resource Center (login required), so the public links above cover the underlying Prebid floors mechanism that Demand Manager builds on rather than the Magnite-specific automation settings.

investigate.intro: Review which inventory runs on a dynamic or automated floor versus a fixed floor, and look for any test result or before-and-after read that compares them.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Open the floor or pricing settings in the Magnite console and note, per piece of supply, whether the floor is a fixed CPM or a dynamic or automated floor.
Record which slices of inventory run on an automated floor and which, if any, run on a fixed floor that could serve as a comparison baseline.

demand_manager.label: Magnite Demand Manager
steps: If Demand Manager manages the wrapper, open its floor controls and note whether the Prebid floors are configured as static (hardcoded) or dynamic (fetched per auction).
Check whether any automated floor guidance is enabled and recording that as the active floor source.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

reporting.label: Reporting (Magnite console or ad server)
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory the floor targets, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average CPM.
Compare a slice running an automated floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the automated floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Note whether the data supports the current floor choice or whether no fixed-floor slice exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per slice of inventory, whether the floor is fixed, dynamic, or automated, and whether any comparison between an automated floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the dynamic-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where automated floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each slice of inventory and whether a dynamic-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs dynamic or automated floor pricing without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between automated and fixed floors has not been tested. A dynamic floor sets the price to maximize yield, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, running the automated floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average price across both before settling on a floor strategy. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.
